# OWID Caption Prompt Iteration — V4 vs V6 vs V7

5 chart images, captioned with three prompt variants:
- **V4**: tight, terse data-story (~160 words, no layout prefix)
- **V6**: title + subtitle layout + ~270-word body
- **V7**: V6 + chart-note + legend + axis text incorporation, hedge recap

---

## Mental health and substance use disorders death rate by age group
`death-rates-from-mental-and-substance-disorders-by-age-who`

<img src="data:image/png;base64," width="700" />

### V4 (117w)

In 2021, the estimated annual number of deaths from mental health and substance use disorders per 100,000 people varied significantly across age groups. The death rate for the 50-69 age group was the highest at 9.2, followed by the 70+ age group at 6.9. The 15-49 age group had a death rate of 4.5, while the overall rate for all ages was 4.4. Death rates for younger age groups were notably lower, with the 5-14 age group at less than 0.1 and the 0-4 age group at 0. The data shows a clear pattern where death rates from these disorders increase with age, peaking in the 50-69 age group before declining slightly in the oldest age category.

### V6 (282w)

Mental health and substance use disorders death rate by age group, World, 2021

Estimated annual number of deaths by age group from mental health and substance use disorders per 100,000 people in the same age group. These include alcohol use, drug use, and eating disorders. Figures do not include deaths resulting from suicide, which are related to these disorders.

Across the global population in 2021, the estimated annual death rate from mental health and substance use disorders varies significantly by age group, measured as deaths per 100,000 people within each cohort. The horizontal bar chart displays six distinct age categories, ranging from early childhood to the oldest demographic. The lowest mortality rates appear in the youngest groups: the 0-4 years category records a rate of 0, and the 5-14 years group shows a rate of less than 0.1 deaths per 100,000. Moving into adolescence and adulthood, the 15-49 years age group registers a rate of 4.5, which sits slightly above the aggregate figure for all ages combined, recorded at 4.4. The data shows a sharp increase in mortality among older populations. The 70+ years category reaches a rate of 6.9 deaths per 100,000. The highest value on the chart belongs to the 50-69 years age group, which records a rate of 9.2 deaths per 100,000, more than double the global average across all ages. This distribution places the peak mortality burden in the middle-to-late adult years rather than exclusively in the oldest age bracket. The visual arrangement of the bars highlights a non-linear progression where risk remains negligible in childhood, rises to moderate levels in young adulthood, and peaks in the decades preceding old age before declining slightly in the 70+ cohort.

### V7 (290w)

Mental health and substance use disorders death rate by age group, World, 2021

Estimated annual number of deaths by age group from mental health and substance use disorders per 100,000 people in the same age group. These include alcohol use, drug use, and eating disorders. Figures do not include deaths resulting from suicide, which are related to these disorders.

The horizontal bar chart displays the estimated annual death rate per 100,000 people attributed to mental health and substance use disorders across six distinct age groups globally in 2021. The data encompasses fatalities related to alcohol use, drug use, and eating disorders, while explicitly excluding deaths resulting from suicide. The vertical axis lists age cohorts ranging from early childhood to the elderly, while the horizontal bars extend to represent the magnitude of the death rate. The distribution shows a clear concentration of mortality in older age brackets, with the 50-69 years group recording the highest rate at 9.2 deaths per 100,000 people. The 70+ years age group follows with the second-highest value of 6.9. In contrast, the rate for the 15-49 years demographic stands at 4.5, which aligns closely with the aggregate figure for all ages at 4.4. Mortality figures drop precipitously for younger populations; the 5-14 years group registers a rate of less than 0.1, and the 0-4 years group shows a value of 0. The visual arrangement highlights a steep gradient where death rates remain negligible in childhood, rise to moderate levels in young and middle adulthood, and peak within the 50-69 years bracket before declining slightly in the oldest cohort. The data shows that the burden of death from these specific disorders is most heavily concentrated in the middle-aged and elderly populations compared to children and young adults.

---

## Rate of child deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition
`rate-of-child-deaths-attributed-to-child-and-maternal-malnutrition`

<img src="data:image/png;base64," width="700" />

### V4 (161w)

Across the globe in 2023, the rate of child deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition varies significantly by region, measured as the estimated number of deaths per 100,000 children under the age of 5. The highest rates, falling in the darkest red bands representing over 1,000 deaths per 100,000, are concentrated in a contiguous belt across sub-Saharan Africa, including nations such as Nigeria, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, and Somalia. Moderate rates, visible in the orange and lighter red shades corresponding to 50 to 500 deaths per 100,000, appear across parts of South Asia, including India and Pakistan, as well as in pockets of Central America and the Middle East. Conversely, the lightest beige colors, indicating rates below 10 deaths per 100,000, cover the majority of North America, Europe, Australia, and the southern cone of South America. The geographic distribution shows a clear concentration of high mortality rates in the African continent compared to the rest of the world.

### V6 (284w)

Rate of child deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition, 2023

The estimated number of deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition, per 100,000 children under the age of 5.

The choropleth map displays the estimated rate of child deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition per 100,000 children under the age of 5 across the globe in 2023. A continuous color scale ranging from light beige to deep maroon indicates the magnitude of mortality, with the legend marking thresholds at 0, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500, 1,000, and 2,000 deaths. The highest values appear concentrated in a band across sub-Saharan Africa, where countries including Chad, the Central African Republic, Somalia, and Mali fall into the darkest maroon band representing rates exceeding 1,000 deaths per 100,000 children. Surrounding nations in West and East Africa, such as Nigeria, Ethiopia, and Sudan, show elevated rates in the 200 to 1,000 range, depicted in medium to dark red tones. In South Asia, Afghanistan and Pakistan display rates in the 100 to 500 band, while India and Bangladesh appear in lighter orange shades corresponding to the 50 to 200 range. Most of Latin America, including Brazil and Mexico, along with parts of Southeast Asia like Indonesia, fall within the 20 to 100 band. The lowest rates, shown in pale beige, cover nearly all of North America, Europe, Australia, and East Asian nations like China and Japan, where values sit below 20 deaths per 100,000 children. Greenland and a few small island states are marked as having no data. The geographic distribution shows a clear concentration of high mortality rates in specific regions of Africa and parts of Asia, while low rates dominate the Global North and Oceania.

### V7 (306w)

Rate of child deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition, 2023

The estimated number of deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition, per 100,000 children under the age of 5.

This choropleth map displays the estimated mortality rate for children under five attributed to malnutrition across the globe in 2023, measured as deaths per 100,000 children. The visualization employs a continuous color scale ranging from light beige to deep maroon, where darker shades correspond to higher mortality rates. The legend defines specific bands starting at 0 and extending through 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500, 1,000, and up to 2,000 deaths per 100,000. The highest values, represented by the darkest maroon colors falling in the bands above 1,000, are concentrated in a contiguous region of sub-Saharan Africa. Countries including Chad, the Central African Republic, Somalia, and Nigeria appear in these darkest bands, indicating rates exceeding 1,000 deaths per 100,000 children. Surrounding nations in West and East Africa, such as Mali, Burkina Faso, and Ethiopia, display slightly lighter but still intense red shades, placing them in the 500 to 1,000 range. In contrast, the lowest rates are visible across North America, Western Europe, Australia, and parts of South America like Chile and Argentina, where the map shows light beige colors corresponding to the 0 to 10 band. Intermediate values appear in parts of South Asia and Southeast Asia; India and Pakistan show orange hues consistent with the 50 to 200 range, while Indonesia and the Philippines fall in lighter orange bands between 20 and 50. Greenland and several island nations are marked with diagonal hatching, indicating no data is available for these territories. The geographic distribution shows a clear concentration of the highest mortality rates in the central belt of the African continent, while the lowest rates cluster in high-income regions of the Northern Hemisphere and Oceania.

---

## Number of people living in extreme poverty
`above-or-below-extreme-poverty-line-world-bank`

<img src="data:image/png;base64," width="700" />

### V4 (148w)

Between 1990 and 2026, the global population living in extreme poverty has declined significantly while the total world population has grown. The chart uses a stacked area format to show two distinct groups: those living below the International Poverty Line of $3 per day, represented by the lower red band, and those not in extreme poverty, represented by the upper orange band. In 1990, the number of people in extreme poverty stood at approximately 2.3 billion. This figure remained relatively stable through the 1990s before beginning a consistent downward trajectory after 2000. By 2026, the red area contracts to a level below 1 billion. Conversely, the orange area expands continuously throughout the timeline, rising from roughly 3 billion in 1990 to over 7 billion in 2026. The visualization shows a clear divergence where the population not in extreme poverty grows rapidly while the population in extreme poverty shrinks.

### V6 (286w)

Number of people living in extreme poverty, World, 1990 to 2026

Extreme poverty is defined as living below the International Poverty Line of $3 per day. This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

The stacked area chart tracks the global population count from 1990 to 2026, separating individuals into two distinct categories: those living in extreme poverty and those not in extreme poverty. The vertical axis measures the number of people in billions, ranging from 0 to 9 billion, while the horizontal axis marks time in five-year increments. The lower section, shaded in dark pink, represents the population living in extreme poverty. In 1990, this group stands at roughly 2.3 billion people. The line remains relatively flat through the mid-1990s before beginning a steady descent. By 2010, the number drops to approximately 1.5 billion. The decline continues through 2015 and 2020, reaching a level below 1 billion by the end of the timeline in 2026. The upper section, shaded in light orange, represents the population not in extreme poverty. This area expands significantly over the period. In 1990, the total global population is around 5.3 billion, meaning the non-poor population is roughly 3 billion. As the total population rises to over 8 billion by 2026, the non-poor segment grows to occupy the vast majority of the chart area, exceeding 7 billion people. The visual gap between the two groups widens continuously, showing a shift in the global distribution where the majority of population growth occurs outside the extreme poverty category. The data shows a consistent reduction in the absolute number of people living in extreme poverty alongside a substantial increase in the number of people living above the poverty line.

### V7 (280w)

Number of people living in extreme poverty, World, 1990 to 2026

Extreme poverty is defined as living below the International Poverty Line of $3 per day. This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This stacked area chart tracks the global population distribution between those living in extreme poverty and those not, spanning from 1990 to 2026. The vertical axis measures the number of people in billions, while the horizontal axis marks time in five-year increments. The lower section, shaded in dark pink, represents the population living in extreme poverty, while the upper section, shaded in light orange, represents the population not in extreme poverty. In 1990, the number of people living in extreme poverty stood at approximately 2.3 billion. This figure remained relatively stable through the mid-1990s before beginning a consistent downward trajectory. By 2010, the count had fallen to roughly 1.5 billion. The decline continued through 2015 and 2020, reaching a level below 1 billion by the end of the observed period. Conversely, the population not living in extreme poverty shows a continuous upward trend throughout the entire timeline. Starting at roughly 3 billion in 1990, this group expanded to approximately 6 billion by 2010 and is projected to exceed 7 billion by 2026. The total height of the chart rises steadily, indicating overall global population growth during these decades. The data is expressed in international dollars at 2021 prices and relates to income measured after taxes and benefits or to consumption per capita. The visual gap between the two colored areas widens significantly over time, showing that the share of the global population escaping extreme poverty has grown alongside the total population.

---

## Number of deaths by age
`annual-deaths-by-age`

<img src="data:image/png;base64," width="700" />

### V4 (160w)

Global mortality in 2023 follows a distinct U-shaped distribution across age groups, with the highest death counts concentrated in early childhood and late adulthood. The 0-4 age group records the largest number of deaths among the youngest cohorts at 4.825 million, followed by a steady decline through the school-age years to a low of 673,000 in the 15-19 bracket. From age 20 onward, the bars lengthen progressively, crossing the one-million mark in the 30-34 group and rising to a peak of 6.807 million in the 80-84 age range. The 75-79 and 70-74 groups follow closely with 6.578 million and 6.591 million deaths respectively, while the 85-89 group drops to 5.958 million. Mortality figures continue to decrease in the oldest brackets, falling to 3.644 million for ages 90-94 and 243,400 for those aged 100 and above. The data shows a clear concentration of deaths in the 70-84 age window, with the 80-84 group representing the single highest value on the chart.

### V6 (273w)

Number of deaths by age, World, 2023
The estimated number of deaths in each age group. Estimates may be uncertain for countries with poor vital registration systems.

This horizontal bar chart displays the estimated global count of deaths across twenty-five distinct five-year age intervals for the year 2023, ranging from infants aged 0-4 years to individuals aged 100 years and older. The data reveals a distinct U-shaped distribution where mortality is high in early childhood, drops significantly during adolescence and young adulthood, and then rises steadily through middle and old age. The highest volume of deaths occurs in the 80-84 year bracket, totaling 6.807 million, followed closely by the 70-74 year group with 6.591 million and the 75-79 year group with 6.578 million. The 65-69 year cohort also registers a high figure at 5.749 million. At the opposite end of the age spectrum, the 0-4 year group shows a substantial number of deaths at 4.825 million, which is higher than the counts for the 60-64 year group (4.483 million) and the 90-94 year group (3.644 million). The lowest mortality figures appear in the younger adult and teenage years; the 15-19 year group records 673,000 deaths, the 20-24 year group records 864,000, and the 25-29 year group records 927,500. The 10-14 year group sits at 453,900, representing the lowest value among the labeled bars. As age increases beyond 50, the bars lengthen consistently, with the 55-59 year group reaching 3.592 million and the 85-89 year group reaching 5.958 million. The distribution shows a clear concentration of deaths in the older age brackets, with the 70-84 year range containing the longest bars on the chart.

### V7 (283w)

Number of deaths by age, World, 2023

The estimated number of deaths in each age group. Estimates may be uncertain for countries with poor vital registration systems.

This horizontal bar chart displays the estimated number of deaths worldwide in 2023, categorized by five-year age groups ranging from 0-4 years up to 100+ years. The data reveals a distinct U-shaped distribution where mortality is highest at the extremes of the human lifespan and lowest during young adulthood. The largest number of deaths occurs in the 75-79 age group, with 6.578 million recorded fatalities, followed closely by the 80-84 age group at 6.807 million and the 70-74 age group at 6.591 million. Mortality figures remain elevated in the 85-89 age bracket at 5.958 million and the 65-69 bracket at 5.749 million. A separate, significant peak appears at the beginning of life, where the 0-4 age group accounts for 4.825 million deaths, a figure higher than any age group between 5 and 59 years. In contrast, the middle of the chart shows a pronounced dip in mortality; the 10-14 age group records 453,900 deaths, representing the lowest value among all categories listed. Deaths begin to rise again in the 15-19 age group with 673,000 and continue increasing through the 20-24 and 25-29 groups. At the upper end of the scale, the 90-94 age group shows 3.644 million deaths, while the 95-99 group drops to 1.306 million. The 100+ years category records the second-lowest total at 243,400 deaths. The figures exclude international aviation incidents, and estimates may be uncertain for countries with poor vital registration systems. The distribution shows a concentration of mortality in the 70-84 age range alongside a substantial burden of death in early childhood.

---

## Financial support provided through the Green Climate Fund
`green-climate-gcf-fund-pledges`

<img src="data:image/png;base64," width="700" />

### V4 (130w)

Between 2011 and 2022, the total financial support provided through the Green Climate Fund for climate change mitigation and adaptation shows a general upward trajectory. The line begins in 2011 at just under $30 billion and remains flat through 2012. From 2012 to 2016, the value rises steadily to approximately $52 billion. A slight dip occurs in 2017, followed by a recovery in 2018 to roughly $56 billion. The data plateaus between 2019 and 2020, hovering near $53 billion. From 2020 to 2022, the line exhibits its steepest incline, climbing past $60 billion and $70 billion to reach a peak of $80 billion in the final year. The chart records the highest value in 2022, which is more than double the funding level observed at the start of the period.

### V6 (246w)

Financial support provided through the Green Climate Fund, 2011 to 2022
The total financial support provided to developing countries for climate change mitigation and adaptation. Countries have set a collective goal of mobilizing $100 billion per year from 2020 onwards.

This line chart tracks the annual total of financial support provided through the Green Climate Fund from 2011 to 2022, measured in current US dollars. The vertical axis ranges from $0 to $80 billion, with gridlines marking $10 billion increments. The single line labeled "World" begins in 2011 at a value just under $30 billion and remains flat through 2012. A visible rise occurs in 2013, where the line crosses the $40 billion mark. The upward trajectory continues through 2016, reaching a local peak slightly above $50 billion. A dip appears in 2017, bringing the value back below $50 billion, followed by a recovery in 2018 to approximately $55 billion. The line shows a slight decline in 2019 and remains flat through 2020 at a level just above $50 billion. From 2020 to 2022, the chart displays a steep upward slope, with the line crossing $60 billion in 2021 and ending at the series maximum of $80 billion in 2022. The data shows a general increase in funding over the twelve-year period, with the most rapid growth occurring in the final two years. The 2022 value sits at the top edge of the chart's vertical range, representing the highest recorded annual total in the visible timeframe.

### V7 (288w)

Financial support provided through the Green Climate Fund, 2011 to 2022
The total financial support provided to developing countries for climate change mitigation and adaptation. Countries have set a collective goal of mobilizing $100 billion per year from 2020 onwards.

This line chart tracks the annual flow of financial support provided through the Green Climate Fund to developing countries for climate change mitigation and adaptation, measured in current US dollars per year. The vertical axis spans from $0 to $80 billion, while the horizontal axis covers the period from 2011 to 2022. The data series, labeled "World," begins in 2011 at a level just below $30 billion and remains nearly flat through 2012. A distinct upward shift occurs in 2013, where the line rises to approximately $41 billion. The trajectory continues to climb steadily over the next three years, reaching roughly $52 billion in 2016. A minor dip is visible in 2017, dropping the value slightly below $50 billion, followed by a recovery in 2018 that pushes the total to about $56 billion. The figures show a period of relative stability between 2019 and 2020, hovering around the $53 billion mark. From 2020 onwards, the chart displays a steep and continuous ascent, with the line crossing the $60 billion threshold in 2021 and terminating at its highest point in 2022, reaching the $80 billion mark. The subtitle notes that countries have set a collective goal of mobilizing $100 billion per year from 2020 onwards, providing context for the scale of these flows relative to international targets. The most prominent feature of the visualization is the accelerated rate of growth in the final two years, which more than doubles the funding levels seen at the start of the decade.

---
